summary.prompt:
For each paper in this notebook, complete these fields.
Use the EXACT paper title as it appears in the source.
Format strictly as shown — no markdown, no bullets:

PAPER: [exact title]
SUMMARY: [2 sentences]
ADVANTAGE: [key strength] | [key strength]
LIMITATION: [key weakness] | [key weakness]
FUTURE_WORK: [one direction] | [one direction]
===

Rules:
- If a field has no content, write NONE.
- Do not add any text before the first PAPER: line.
- Do not add any text after the last ===.
- Repeat the block for EVERY paper in the notebook.
summary.answer:
PAPER: Toward Self-Improvement of LLMs via Imagination, Searching, and Criticizing
SUMMARY: This paper introduces AlphaLLM, a framework that integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search with large language models to establish a self-improving loop without requiring additional human annotations. It overcomes challenges like data scarcity and vast search spaces by synthesizing prompts and formulating text generation as options over a Markov Decision Process.
ADVANTAGE: Significantly enhances large language model performance without requiring additional data annotations | Demonstrates high efficiency and accuracy that is comparable to GPT-4 on mathematical reasoning tasks
LIMITATION: Evaluation is primarily limited to mathematical reasoning tasks where learning signals are clearly defined | Missing external tools critically impedes the outcome reward model capability to effectively assess challenging problems
FUTURE_WORK: NONE
===
PAPER: Multistep Distillation of Diffusion Models via Moment Matching
SUMMARY: This paper presents a novel method for distilling many-step diffusion models into faster, few-step models by matching the conditional expectations of clean data given noisy data along the sampling trajectory. The proposed moment matching approach extends one-step methods to multi-step scenarios, achieving state-of-the-art image generation results that even outperform the original many-step teacher models.
ADVANTAGE: Outperforms both one-step distilled versions and original many-step teacher models | Enables fast generation of high-resolution images directly in image space without autoencoders
LIMITATION: The alternating optimization variant is generally not guaranteed to converge | Automated metrics of image quality used for evaluation are highly imperfect
FUTURE_WORK: Run a full set of human evaluations on the outputs of the distilled models | Further explore the space of algorithms spanned by alternating updates and instant two-batch updates
===
PAPER: Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs
SUMMARY: The authors present Gorilla, a finetuned LLaMA-based model that leverages Retriever-Aware Training to accurately generate API calls from natural language instructions. By integrating with a document retriever, the model effectively adapts to test-time document changes and significantly mitigates the hallucination errors common in standard language models.
ADVANTAGE: Substantially mitigates hallucination errors compared to standard prompting techniques | Flexibly adapts to test-time API documentation updates and version changes
LIMITATION: Augmenting the model with retrieval can sometimes hurt performance during evaluation if the retriever has imperfect recall | Executing every generated code string for validation is impractical due to complex hardware and dependency requirements
FUTURE_WORK: Transition large language models from knowledge-bound models into flexible interfaces that interact with the digital world | NONE
===
PAPER: Refusal in Language Models Is Mediated by a Single Direction
SUMMARY: This research demonstrates that the refusal behavior of safety fine-tuned large language models is mediated by a single, one-dimensional subspace within their residual stream activations. By leveraging this discovery, the authors introduce a weight orthogonalization method that surgically disables refusal mechanisms with minimal impact on the model's other capabilities.
ADVANTAGE: Provides a simple and effective white-box jailbreak method without needing gradient-based optimization | Maintains overall model coherence and general capabilities while successfully bypassing refusal mechanisms
LIMITATION: Findings may not generalize to untested or future frontier models at greater scales | The methodology used to extract the refusal direction relies on heuristics and is likely not optimal
FUTURE_WORK: Improve the methodological extraction of the single refusal direction | Conduct a more comprehensive mechanistic analysis of adversarial suffixes
===
PAPER: Reducing Transformer Key-Value Cache Size with Cross-Layer Attention
SUMMARY: This paper introduces Cross-Layer Attention, a novel transformer architecture modification that reduces key-value cache memory footprint by sharing activations across adjacent layers. Experimental results show this method delivers a twofold reduction in cache size while maintaining nearly the same accuracy as traditional Multi-Query Attention configurations.
ADVANTAGE: Provides a Pareto improvement over the memory and accuracy tradeoffs of traditional Multi-Query Attention | Operates orthogonally to existing techniques and can be easily combined with Multi-Query or Grouped-Query Attention
LIMITATION: Adapted models fail to fully recover the performance of their original base models after adaptation training | Does not directly evaluate end-to-end inference efficiency consequences on full-scale serving systems
FUTURE_WORK: Evaluate end-to-end inference efficiency of large and long-context models employing Cross-Layer Attention | Improve the adaptation recipe for converting pre-trained models to use Cross-Layer Attention
===

techniques.prompt:
For each paper in this notebook, list technical methods.
Use the EXACT paper title as it appears in the source.
Format strictly as shown — no markdown, no bullets:

PAPER: [exact title]
INTRODUCES: [new method or model name] | [name]
USES: [existing method the paper builds on] | [name]
===

Rules:
- INTRODUCES = novel contributions the paper presents.
- USES = existing prior work methods the paper applies.
- Use short technical names, not full sentences.
- If none, write NONE.
- Repeat the block for EVERY paper in the notebook.
techniques.answer:
PAPER: Toward Self-Improvement of LLMs via Imagination, Searching, and Criticizing
INTRODUCES: AlphaLLM | imagination-searching-criticizing framework | ηMCTS | option-level formulation
USES: Large Language Models | Monte Carlo Tree Search | Markov Decision Process | Chain-of-Thought prompting
===
PAPER: Multistep Distillation of Diffusion Models via Moment Matching
INTRODUCES: Moment Matching Distillation | alternating optimization | parameter-space moment matching
USES: Diffusion Models | Generalized Method of Moments | ancestral sampling | classifier-free guidance
===
PAPER: Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs
INTRODUCES: Gorilla | Retriever-Aware Training | APIBench | AST sub-tree matching
USES: Large Language Models | LLaMA | self-instruct | BM25 | GPT-Index
===
PAPER: Refusal in Language Models Is Mediated by a Single Direction
INTRODUCES: weight orthogonalization | white-box jailbreak method | directional ablation
USES: Chat Models | difference-in-means | activation addition | adversarial suffixes | direct feature attribution
===
PAPER: Reducing Transformer Key-Value Cache Size with Cross-Layer Attention
INTRODUCES: Cross-Layer Attention | CLA2 | CLA3
USES: Transformers | Multi-Query Attention | Grouped-Query Attention | Multi-Head Attention | RMSNorm
===

datasets.prompt:
For each paper in this notebook, list every dataset used in experiments.
Use the EXACT paper title as it appears in the source.
Format strictly as shown — one DATASET line per dataset:

PAPER: [exact title]
DATASET: [dataset name] :: [what task or metric it evaluates]
===

Rules:
- Use the canonical dataset name (e.g. ImageNet, not 'the image benchmark').
- If no datasets are mentioned, write: DATASET: NONE
- Repeat the block for EVERY paper in the notebook.
datasets.answer:
PAPER: Toward Self-Improvement of LLMs via Imagination, Searching, and Criticizing
DATASET: GSM8K :: predicting answers correctly for mathematical reasoning [1]
DATASET: MATH :: predicting answers correctly for mathematical reasoning [1]
===

DATASET: MS-COCO :: zero-shot image generation measured by FID and CLIP Score [4]
===

DATASET: APIBench :: API functionality accuracy and hallucination errors [5, 6]
===

DATASET: JAILBREAKBENCH :: bypassing refusal on harmful instructions [7, 8]
DATASET: ADVBENCH :: harmful instructions [9]

DATASET: TDC2023 :: harmful instructions [9]
DATASET: HARMBENCH :: attack success rate on standard behaviors [10]
DATASET: ALPACA :: inducing refusal on harmless instructions [9, 11]
DATASET: MMLU :: general language model capabilities [12]
DATASET: ARC :: general language model capabilities [12]
DATASET: GSM8K :: general language model capabilities [12]
DATASET: TRUTHFULQA :: general language model capabilities [12]
DATASET: WINOGRANDE :: general language model capabilities [13]
DATASET: TINYHELLASWAG :: general language model capabilities [13]

PAPER: Reducing Transformer Key-Value Cache Size with Cross-Layer Attention
DATASET: SlimPajama :: pretraining validation perplexity [14, 15]

DATASET: Hellaswag :: downstream benchmark accuracy [18]

DATASET: ARC-E :: downstream benchmark accuracy [18]

categories.prompt:
Assign research category tags and methodology labels to each paper.
Use the EXACT paper title as it appears in the source.
Format strictly as shown — no markdown, no bullets:

PAPER: [exact title]
CATEGORIES: [tag] | [tag]
METHODOLOGY: [approach name] | [approach name]
===

Rules:
- CATEGORIES must come ONLY from this list:
  LLM | Vision | Multimodal | Agentic-AI | Safety | Efficiency |
  NLP | RL | Theory | Graph | Biomedical | Robotics | Code |
  Retrieval | Generative
- METHODOLOGY = high-level methodological approach (e.g. 'Fine-tuning',
  'Mechanistic interpretability', 'Knowledge distillation').
- 1–3 values per field. If unsure write NONE.
- Repeat the block for EVERY paper in the notebook.
categories.answer:
PAPER: Toward Self-Improvement of LLMs via Imagination, Searching, and Criticizing
CATEGORIES: LLM | RL | Agentic-AI
METHODOLOGY: Monte Carlo Tree Search | Reward modeling
===
PAPER: Multistep Distillation of Diffusion Models via Moment Matching
CATEGORIES: Vision | Generative | Efficiency
METHODOLOGY: Knowledge distillation | Moment matching
===
PAPER: Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs
CATEGORIES: LLM | Code | Retrieval
METHODOLOGY: Fine-tuning | Retriever-Aware Training
===
PAPER: Refusal in Language Models Is Mediated by a Single Direction
CATEGORIES: LLM | Safety
METHODOLOGY: Mechanistic interpretability | Activation steering
===
PAPER: Reducing Transformer Key-Value Cache Size with Cross-Layer Attention
CATEGORIES: LLM | Efficiency
METHODOLOGY: Architectural modification | Pre-training
===

use_cases.prompt:
For each paper in this notebook, describe practical use cases.
Use the EXACT paper title as it appears in the source.
Format strictly as shown — no markdown, no bullets:

PAPER: [exact title]
USE_CASE: [concrete 1-sentence application]
USE_CASE: [second application if applicable]
===

Rules:
- USE_CASE lines describe real-world applications, not research contributions.
- Write at least 1 and at most 3 USE_CASE lines per paper.
- Do not repeat the method name — describe the downstream use.
- Repeat the block for EVERY paper in the notebook.
use_cases.answer:
PAPER: Toward Self-Improvement of LLMs via Imagination, Searching, and Criticizing
USE_CASE: Providing step-by-step autonomous tutoring for complex mathematical or logic problems in educational software.
USE_CASE: Powering autonomous coding assistants that can iteratively plan, generate, and verify complex software programs without human intervention.
===
PAPER: Multistep Distillation of Diffusion Models via Moment Matching
USE_CASE: Generating high-quality visual assets in real-time for interactive design applications and video game environments.
USE_CASE: Deploying rapid text-to-image generative capabilities on mobile devices or edge hardware with limited computational power.
===
PAPER: Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs
USE_CASE: Creating intelligent developer assistants that automatically write syntactically correct code for frequently updated cloud services.
USE_CASE: Building natural language interfaces that allow non-programmers to orchestrate complex machine learning pipelines and database queries.
===
PAPER: Refusal in Language Models Is Mediated by a Single Direction
USE_CASE: Developing precise safety filters for enterprise chatbots to strictly enforce guardrails against generating harmful or inappropriate content.
USE_CASE: Assisting red-teaming teams to audit and uncover vulnerabilities in the safety mechanisms of newly deployed language models.
===
PAPER: Reducing Transformer Key-Value Cache Size with Cross-Layer Attention
USE_CASE: Processing and summarizing extensive legal documents or entire books by enabling longer context windows on standard hardware.
USE_CASE: Serving conversational AI models to thousands of concurrent users efficiently by supporting significantly larger batch sizes on cloud servers.
===
